title: A Mixed Battle Song
content:
آ آ آ آ Lo! the Boarï؟½s tail is salted, and the Kangarooï؟½s exalted,
آ آ آ آ And his right eye is extinguished by a man-oï؟½-warsmanï؟½s cap;
آ آ آ آ He is flying round the fences where the Southern Sea commences,
آ آ آ آ And heï؟½s very much excited for a quiet sort of chap.
آ آ آ آ For his ships have had a scrap and theyï؟½ve marked it on the map
آ آ آ آ Where the H.M.A.S. Sydney dropped across a German trap.
آ آ آ آ So the Kangarooï؟½s a-chasing of his Blessed Self, and racing
آ آ آ آ From Cape York right round to Leeuwin, from the coast to Nevertire;
آ آ آ آ And of him need be no more said, save that to the tail aforesaid
آ آ آ آ Is the Blue Australian Ensign firmly fixed with copper wire.
آ آ آ آ (When heï؟½s filled the map with white men thereï؟½ll be little to desire.)
آ آ آ آ I was sulky, I was moody (Iï؟½m inclined to being broody)
آ آ آ آ When the news appeared in Sydney, bringing joy and bringing tears,
آ آ آ آ (Thereï؟½s an undertone of sorrow that youï؟½ll understand to-morrow)
آ آ آ آ And I felt a something in me that had not been there for years.
آ آ آ آ Though I lean in the direction of most absolute Protection
آ آ آ آ (And of wheat on the selection)
آ آ آ آ And, considering Congestion and the hopeless unemployed,
آ آ آ آ Iï؟½d a notion (but I hid it) that, the way the Emden did it,
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Twould be better for Australia if her ï؟½commerceï؟½ was destroyed.

آ آ آ آ You may say that warï؟½s a curse, but the peace curse may be worse,
آ آ آ آ When itï؟½s lasted till itï؟½s rotten, rotten from the inmost core,
آ آ آ آ To the mouldy skin which we are, in the land we call the freer, 
آ آ آ آ And I almost feel inclined to call for ï؟½Three Cheers for the War!ï؟½
آ آ آ آ For I think, when all is over, from Magellanï؟½s Straits to Dover,
آ آ آ آ Things will be a great deal better than they ever were before.
آ آ آ آ But, since ï؟½Peaceï؟½ and ï؟½Rightï؟½ are squalling, Iï؟½ll content myself with calling
آ آ آ آ For three rousers, like the ringing cheers we used to give of yore, 
آ آ آ آ For the Emden!
آ آ آ آ For the Sydney!
آ آ آ آ And their gallant crews and captains, both of whom weï؟½ve met before!
آ آ آ آ And, for Kaiser Williamï؟½s nevvy, we shall venture three cheers more!
آ آ آ آ Cheers that go to end a war.
meaning:
This evocative piece by Henry Lawson represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Henry Lawson's bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
